response: Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-abc", "next_review_date": "2026-04-15"}]. The next_review_date parameter directly sets when the project appears for review, overriding the calculated date from last_reviewed + review_interval.
Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-xyz", "completed_by_children": true}]. This enables the project to automatically mark itself complete when its last remaining task is completed.

Call get_tasks with task_id for the recurring task (or query='Weekly team standup'). The response includes nextDueDate, which shows when the next occurrence is due without needing to complete the current one. No tool call needed beyond reading the existing data — just inspect the nextDueDate field in the get_tasks response.

Call get_projects with include_task_health=true. This returns availableCount per project. Filter the results for projects where availableCount is 0 (stalled projects). Alternatively, call get_projects with stalled_only=true to get projects that have no available actions directly.

No tool call needed — this is an explanation question. The behavior depends on the catchUpAutomatically field. Call get_tasks with the task ID to check catchUpAutomatically. If catchUpAutomatically is true, completing the task creates just one catch-up occurrence (the next future due date). If false, each missed interval spawns its own occurrence, so you could get 5 overdue tasks. Check the field value to know which behavior applies.

No tool call strictly needed — this is a knowledge question. The answer depends on whether the 'Priority' parent tag has childrenAreMutuallyExclusive set to true. If it does, assigning 'Low' via add_tags will silently remove 'High' (the sibling). Call get_tags to check the childrenAreMutuallyExclusive property of the 'Priority' parent tag. If true, warn the user that add_tags=['Low'] will automatically remove 'High'. This is by design, not a bug.

Call create_projects with [{"name": "Q3 Report", "folder_path": "Work", "due_date": "2026-07-15", "defer_date": "2026-06-01"}]. The defer_date makes the project hidden/unavailable until June 1, and due_date sets the July 15 deadline.
Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-abc", "due_date": ""}]. Passing an empty string clears the due date.
Call get_projects with project_id="proj-xyz". The response includes dueDate, deferDate, and plannedDate fields.

Call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-101", "parent_task_id": "task-200"}]. This moves the task from the inbox to become a subtask of 'Draft report'. Note: parent_task_id is used (not project_id) because we want it nested under a specific task.
Call get_tasks with query="mortgage". This searches task names and notes case-insensitively across all projects.

Call get_tags (no parameters). The response includes parentTagId for each tag. Find the tag named 'High' and check its parentTagId — then cross-reference that ID to see if it belongs to the 'Energy' tag.
Call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-500", "status": "dropped"}]. This marks the task as dropped/abandoned without deleting it. Note: this task is not recurring, so no need to clear recurrence first.

Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-A", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-B", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-C", "status": "dropped"}]. This batch-drops all three projects without deleting them.

No tool call needed — this is an explanation. Per the server instructions: tasks in a completed project show completed: false but available: false. The task's own completed field reflects only its own state, not its container's. The tasks are individually incomplete but belong to a done project. Use available_only=true in get_tasks to exclude these non-actionable tasks. They appear because get_tasks returns tasks regardless of project state by default.
Call reorder_project with project_id="proj-C", before_project_id="proj-A". This places proj-C before proj-A, making it first in the folder.

Two calls needed. First: create_tags with [{"name": "Priority", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}]. Second: create_tags with [{"name": "High", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Medium", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Low", "parent_tag": "Priority"}]. The parent must exist before creating children, so these must be sequential calls.

Call update_tags with [{"id": "tag-energy-001", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}]. This enables mutual exclusivity on the existing parent tag, so assigning one child energy tag will automatically remove any sibling energy tags.
Call update_projects with [{"id": "proj-website-001", "flagged": true, "add_tags": ["High Priority"]}]. This flags the project and adds the tag in a single call.
Call reorder_task with task_id="task-qa-001", before_task_id="task-fe-001". This moves QA testing before Frontend build in the project task order.
Call delete_tags with tag_ids="tag-errands-001". This permanently deletes the tag. Tasks that had the tag will lose the association but are not deleted themselves.
Call get_perspectives (no parameters). The response includes name, type (built-in/custom), and id. Filter the results for type="custom" to see only custom perspectives.
Call create_folders with [{"name": "Q2", "parent_path": "Work"}]. This creates the Q2 folder nested inside the Work folder.
Call switch_perspective with perspective_name="Weekly Review".

Two steps. First: call get_tasks with overdue=true to retrieve all overdue tasks. Second: after reviewing the results and identifying the 3 most urgent, call update_tasks with [{"id": "<id1>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<id2>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<id3>", "flagged": true}] to flag them.

First call get_projects with query="Quantum Computing Research" to check if the project exists. If found, call get_tasks with project_id=<the project ID>. If not found, inform the user that no such project exists. Could also try get_tasks with query="Quantum Computing Research" directly to search across all projects.
Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Buy birthday gift", "tags": ["Computer", "Errands"]}]. No project_id means it goes to inbox. Tags are passed as a list of strings.

Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Buy groceries"}, {"task_name": "Do laundry"}, {"task_name": "Wash dishes"}, {"task_name": "Vacuum living room"}, {"task_name": "Prep meals for the week"}]. No project_id means all go to inbox.
Call create_projects with [{"name": "Kitchen Renovation", "project_type": "sequential", "folder_path": "Home"}, {"name": "Garden Planning", "project_type": "parallel", "folder_path": "Home"}].

First: call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Event Planning", "project_id": "proj-100", "sequential": true}] to create the parent action group. Then: call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Research venues", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-task-id>"}, {"task_name": "Book venue", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-task-id>"}, {"task_name": "Send invitations", "parent_task_id": "<event-planning-task-id>"}] to add the subtasks. The sequential=true on the parent makes them ordered. Two sequential calls required (need parent ID first).

This requires modeling parallel independent chains. Step 1: Call create_projects with [{"name": "Home Renovation", "project_type": "parallel"}]. Step 2: Create two sequential action groups as tasks in the project. Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Contractor Chain", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "sequential": true}, {"task_name": "Paint Chain", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "sequential": true}]. Step 3: Add subtasks to each chain. For Contractor Chain: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Get contractor quotes", "parent_task_id": "<contractor-chain-id>"}, {"task_name": "Schedule contractor", "parent_task_id": "<contractor-chain-id>"}]. For Paint Chain: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Choose paint colors", "parent_task_id": "<paint-chain-id>"}, {"task_name": "Buy paint", "parent_task_id": "<paint-chain-id>"}]. The parallel project type means both chains are available simultaneously, while sequential action groups enforce ordering within each chain.

Three sequential steps. Step 1: Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Prepare presentation", "project_id": "proj-200", "sequential": true}] to create the parent action group. Step 2: Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Research topic", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Write outline", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>"}, {"task_name": "Create slides", "parent_task_id": "<prepare-id>", "sequential": false}]. Note: 'Create slides' needs sequential=false (parallel) since its subtasks should be parallel. Actually, sequential defaults to false (parallel), so we might not need it explicitly — but setting it makes the intent clear. Step 3: Call create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Design template", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}, {"task_name": "Add content", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}, {"task_name": "Add animations", "parent_task_id": "<create-slides-id>"}]. The parent 'Prepare presentation' is sequential so Research → Write outline → Create slides happen in order, while Create slides' subtasks are parallel.

OmniFocus doesn't support true diamond/converging dependencies (task depending on multiple predecessors across branches). The closest approximation: create a sequential project or action group where 'Draft report' and 'Collect data' are in a parallel sub-group, followed by 'Review findings'. Step 1: Create a sequential parent action group in proj-300: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Report workflow", "project_id": "proj-300", "sequential": true}]. Step 2: Create the parallel prerequisite group and the final task: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Prerequisites", "parent_task_id": "<workflow-id>", "sequential": false}, {"task_name": "Review findings", "parent_task_id": "<workflow-id>"}]. Step 3: Add the two independent tasks as subtasks of the parallel Prerequisites group: create_tasks with [{"task_name": "Draft report", "parent_task_id": "<prereqs-id>"}, {"task_name": "Collect data", "parent_task_id": "<prereqs-id>"}]. This way: Draft report and Collect data are parallel (both available), and the sequential parent blocks 'Review findings' until the Prerequisites group completes (both subtasks done). This correctly models the diamond dependency.

Per the server instructions: dropping a recurring task without clearing recurrence first spawns the next occurrence. To stop the series entirely, call update_tasks with [{"id": "task-weekly-123", "recurrence": "", "status": "dropped"}]. Clearing recurrence first (in the same update) prevents a new occurrence from spawning when the task is dropped.

Call update_tags with [{"id": "tag-aimee-001", "parent_tag": "People"}]. This moves the tag under the 'People' parent. Task associations are preserved automatically — moving a tag in the hierarchy doesn't affect which tasks have that tag.
